entry.title: Custom hierarchy order in Data Analyzer (SAP Analytics Cloud)?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

in SAP Analytics Cloud I can define a custom hierarchy order in a Story when I am in Edit mode.

 

However, in the Data Analyzer I only see the following options for hierarchies:

  • Ascending
  • Descending
  • Default order

My question is:

Is it possible to define a custom hierarchy order directly in the Data Analyzer, similar to what is possible in Story Edit mode?

If not, is there any workaround planned or recommended?

Thanks a lot in advance!

Best regards
T’hami

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Go-Live Success: A Comprehensive Checklist for Driving User Adoption
entry.content:
Implementing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) successfully requires more than just technical deployment—it demands a strategic approach to ensure optimal performance, user adoption, and long-term value. This comprehensive guide outlines the critical areas organizations must address to achieve a successful SAC go-live for both Analytics and Planning scenarios.

The Foundation: Four Pillars of SAC Success

A successful SAC implementation rests on four fundamental pillars that must be carefully evaluated and optimized:

  1. Data Acquisition and Live Connection
  2. Modeling Excellence
  3. Story Design Optimization
  4. Ongoing House Keeping

Let's explore each pillar in detail to understand how they contribute to driving adoption and consumption in your SAC environment.

Pillar 1: Data Acquisition and Live Connection

The foundation of any analytics solution lies in robust data connectivity. For SAC implementations, this means ensuring your infrastructure can handle the demands of real-time analytics and planning scenarios.

Network and Infrastructure Considerations

Your network configuration becomes critical, especially when implementing live connections to source systems. This includes:

  • Device compatibility testing across laptops and mobile devices to ensure consistent user experience
  • Source system capacity evaluation for live scenarios to prevent performance bottlenecks
  • Tenant capacity planning, particularly crucial for import scenarios and planning workloads

End-to-End Connection Validation

A comprehensive review of your SAC connections should cover multiple technical layers:

  • Cloud Connector configuration and optimization
  • Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO) setup
  • User authorization frameworks
  • Browser behavior testing in realistic business scenarios

These elements work together to create a seamless user experience that encourages adoption rather than creating barriers.

Pillar 2: Modeling Excellence

Effective modeling in SAC requires balancing functionality with performance, ensuring your solution serves actual business needs rather than simply showcasing available data.

Requirement Alignment and Simplification

Before diving into complex modeling scenarios, evaluate whether your requirements align with SAC's standard capabilities. Ask yourself:

  • Do your needs fit SAC's out-of-the-box functionality?
  • Are you creating unnecessary workarounds for complex workflows?
  • Is your commenting and large table handling approach sustainable?

Business-Focused Design Philosophy

The most successful SAC implementations follow a principle of designing for the business goal rather than building from all available data. This approach involves:

  • Keeping models simple and efficient by using only truly necessary data
  • Reducing data volume early in the modeling process
  • Avoiding unnecessary complexity that can hinder maintenance and scaling
  • Designing for maintainability to ensure long-term success

SAC Best Practices Implementation

Following established SAC best practices ensures your solution is secure, reliable, and provides smooth workflows. Key areas include:

  • Proper date handling and hierarchy design
  • Robust access control implementation
  • Comprehensive validation frameworks
  • Optimized planning area configuration

Pillar 3: Story Design Optimization

User experience in SAC stories directly impacts adoption rates. Poor performance or clunky interfaces can quickly derail even the most well-intentioned analytics initiatives.

Script and Event Optimization

Pay special attention to story pages utilizing scripts in critical events:

  • Review "onInitialization" and "onActive" event implementations
  • Eliminate unnecessary widget refreshes that can slow down user interactions
  • Optimize long-running data actions and multi-actions for better performance

Performance Testing and Validation

Comprehensive performance testing should cover both individual and concurrent usage scenarios:

  • Single-user performance optimization to ensure responsive individual experiences
  • High user concurrency testing to validate system behavior under realistic load conditions
  • Realistic scenario and volume testing to identify potential bottlenecks before go-live

Pillar 4: House Keeping and Ongoing Maintenance

Successful SAC implementations require ongoing attention to system health and optimization opportunities.

System Monitoring and Health Checks

Regular monitoring of your SAC environment should include:

  • Homepage System Overview reviews to identify potential issues early
  • Import job failure monitoring to ensure data freshness and reliability
  • Data action performance tracking to identify optimization opportunities
  • Long-running query analysis to prevent user experience degradation

Job Optimization and Scheduling

Efficient job management contributes significantly to overall system performance:

  • Review calendar job cadence regularly
  • Adjust or reduce job frequency where possible without impacting business requirements
  • Balance system resource utilization across different time periods

Driving Adoption Through Excellence

The ultimate goal of following this comprehensive checklist is to create an SAC environment that users want to engage with regularly. When data connections are reliable, models are intuitive, stories perform well, and the system runs smoothly, user adoption naturally follows.

Organizations that systematically address each of these four pillars typically see higher user engagement, better business outcomes, and stronger return on their SAC investment. The key is treating go-live not as a destination, but as the beginning of an ongoing optimization journey.

Next Steps for Your SAC Implementation

As you prepare for your SAC go-live or optimize an existing implementation, consider conducting a comprehensive review across all four pillars. Start with the areas that most directly impact your users' daily experience, then work systematically through the technical infrastructure elements that support those experiences. If you seek expert services and guidance for any of the areas please check our engagement plans and service portfolio.

Remember, successful SAC adoption is built on the foundation of technical excellence, user-centric design, and ongoing operational discipline. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on these core principles, your organization can maximize the value of its SAC investment while driving meaningful business outcomes through enhanced analytics and planning capabilities.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: Re: Issue with AI160 JavaScript Exercise – /api/agent/tri...
entry.content:
This question was resolved in GitHub issue #26.

The problem was a port conflict with SAP Business Application Studio (3000/3001). The application already occupies those ports, so the UI proxy hits an HTML error page instead of the agent route.

The latest version of the demo on GitHub improves the error messaging to make port conflicts more obvious.

To resolve the conflict, update the ports used by the mock server and agent server.

  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/mock-server/src/server.ts file to set the mock server port to 3005 (or another free port).
// exercises/javascript/app/mock-server/src/server.ts
-const port = process.env.PORT || 3001;
+const port = process.env.PORT || 3005;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/setup.ts file to point the base URL to port 3005.
// exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/setup.ts
export const BASE_URL_API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV =
`http://localhost:3005/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV/`;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/server.ts file to set the agent server port to 3006 (or another free port).
// exercises/javascript/app/agent/src/server.ts
-const port = process.env.PORT || 3001;
+const port = process.env.PORT || 3006;
  1. Update the exercises/javascript/app/ui/vite.config.ts file to point the UI proxy to port 3006.
// exercises/javascript/app/ui/vite.config.ts
- target: 'http://localhost:3001',
+ target: 'http://localhost:3006',

After these changes, the preview endpoint should route correctly, and the agent call should stop returning “HTML/404”.

entry.title: Re: Can I resell Crystal Reports purchased from the SAP S...
entry.content: Dear customer, no you cannot purchase on SAP Store and resell to another company. The buyer needs to be the user and the payer. SAP Store is direct sales only. For OEM agreements please contact the SAP Partner team. Kind regards. Dominik

entry.title: Staging in SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) – An Introduction to "Config Push"
entry.content:
Introduction

Motivation and goal

In software development, it is standard procedure to use staging for developed content, running it through quality assurance (QAS) before pushing it to the production system (PROD). This avoids the risk of developing directly in the production environment.

This key functionality has been missing in the Advanced Event Mesh (AEM), leading to the problem that developing directly in the production system is dangerous, and manually copying every event from one AEM to another is tedious.

This problem is solved with the new "Config Push" functionality, which is the main topic of this blog post. 
In the next few minutes, you will get the long-awaited answer to the question:

"How can you effectively implement staging in the AEM?"

This blog seeks to enable you to fully utilize this new functionality and profit from a more robust and less tedious AEM architectural landscape that now natively supports staging.

Covered content

In the first chapter, a brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal will be given to allow everyone to fully understand the new functionality. If you're already familiar with the AEM and the Event Portal, you can skip ahead to the second chapter, where a step-by-step tutorial on how to enable and use the functionality is provided. If you have limited time or just want to get a quick glimpse of the new functionality, you can also jump ahead to the last chapter, where the most important aspects and steps will be summarized.


Brief overview of the AEM and the Event Portal

AEM supports many key features that come along with the broker and the entitlement like the Event Portal, Micro-Integrations, Mission Control and Insights.
Overview of functionality
The key feature this blog focuses on is the Event Portal, a tool used for designing your events. To use it effectively, you should first consider your business processes that you have and then build events based on them. This design-first approach is developer friendly, as it allows you to create and refine all your events before pushing them to the system.
 
Designed Runtime in the Event Portal Designer
The Event Portal Designer also gives you a full holistic view of the ecosystem. Within the components view all the consumed applications and events are displayed and you are able to directly add subscribers.
Component view
It enables you to see which which applications publish and subscribe an event.
Event details
You can also view the details from an application's perspective.
Application Details
Now the question is, once you designed the runtime, how can you have a lifecycle version control in place and push all the created controls to the broker?
This question is answered by the new functionality "Config Push".
 

How to enable staging by using "Config Push"

Once you have a designed Application Domain, you can now push it to the running broker. 
All the available AEM brokers can be found under the "Cluster Manager" tab.
AEM Brokers in the cluster manager

 

Push applications to DEV broker

  1. First you have to have created a runtime and connect it to the broker
    1. Go to Runtime Manager
      1. Runtime manager 
    2. Click on "Create Modeled Event Mesh" at the top right
      1. Create modeled event mesh modal
    3. Click on the new Modeled Event Mesh 
      1. Open modeled Event Mesh
    4. Go to the "Event Broker Connections Tab"
      1. Event broker connections tab
    5. Click on "Connect Event Broker" and select the broker you want to connect to
      1. Connect event broker
    6. It should now look like this. Every push that will be done is now against this broker
      1. Event Broker Connections
    7. Under "Architecture" all the objects that will be pushed are displayed
      1. Architecture tab
  2. Next you have to add the Applications to the runtime.
    1. Go again into the Designer and click on your Application Domain
      1. Application Domain
    2. Click on the Application that you want to push and open the application details (can also be done via the components view)
    3. Add the runtime and broker in the configuration of the application 
      1. Configuration
      2. Select the broker that is also connected to the runtime
        1. Select broker
      3. Define the client credentials
        1. Set client credentials
        2. Client Credentials can be found in the Cluster Manager > Broker > Try Me
          1. Find client credentials
      4. Click "Save & Promote" Later
        1. Save and promote later
    4. Promote Application
      1. Go to "Promotions", click on "Promote Version" and select the broker
      2. Click on "Preview Promotion"
        1.  
      3. Click on "Promote"
    5. The pushed application should now have a checkmark. Now check that the application was deployed by navigating to the previously created runtime under Runtime Manager > Runtime > Architecture. The deployed application should now be displayed
  3. Congratulations. You now have pushed your first application to the dev environment. You can now redo steps 2.1 to 2.4 for the other applications that you want to push.

 

Push applications to the other environment brokers

To push your applications to other subaccounts, your landscape must contain multiple subaccounts with the AEM that have a one to one connection. One subaccount for development (DEV), one subaccount for quality assurance (QAS) and one subaccount for production (PROD). Note that this blog only demonstrates linking to the PROD environment.
 
With the "Config Push" feature, you can push your previously promoted applications to the other environments, such as QAS or PROD, directly from the DEV AEM Event Portal. This is possible even though the subaccounts are normally isolated and unaware of one another.
  1. First you have to set up the subaccount landscape by linking the secondary AEM subaccounts to the main DEV subaccount 
    1. PROD: Go to your PROD Subaccount and into Account Details > Account Settings
      1. PROD: Enable linking
      2. (DEV: When trying to enable it in the DEV subaccount it is disabled, as in the subaccount that is linked to another subaccount no designing should be taking place. All the designing should be done in the DEV subaccount. Once you enable the "Subaccount Linking" the Event Portal will be disabled)
        1.  DEV: Enable Linking
    2. PROD: Click on "Create Link Code"
      1. PROD: Create Link Code
    3. PROD: Click on "Copy"
      1. PROD: Copy
      2. DEV: Paste link code in the Event Portal subaccount
        1. DEV: Go to DEV subaccount AEM Environments Page
          1. DEV: Environment Page
        2. DEV: Create new Environment
          1. DEV: Create environment
        3. DEV: Select "For brokers in a secondary AEM subaccount
          1. DEV: select for secondary
        4. DEV: Name it accordingly and select "Production" if its the Production subaccount
          1. DEV: Select production
        5. DEV: Click on "Link Now", paste the "Link Code" that you copied previously and click on "Link"
          1. DEV: Paste and link
        6. DEV: Now you can create a "Modeled Event Mesh" by navigating to the Runtime Event Manager for the subaccount
          1. DEV: Create Modeled Event Mesh
        7. DEV: After that you only have to click on the Modeled Event Mesh, go to "Event Broker Connections", click on "Connect Event Broker" and connect the production broker
          1. DEV: Connect Event Broker
  2. Now you have set up the subaccount landscape, you can push your promoted applications and events to other stages!
 

Summary 

This blog introduced "Config Push," a new capability for the AEM designed to streamline configuration management across multiple environments. It provides a comprehensive overview of how to leverage the Event Portal to design and promote application configurations. The result is a more robust workflow for managing the AEM landscape.
 
The core process involves these four steps:
  1. Establish the Development Environment: Create a "Modeled Event Mesh" within the Runtime Manager and connect it to the designated development broker.
  2. Deploy to Development: Configure the target application within the Event Portal Designer, associate it with the development runtime, and use the "Promote" function to deploy it.
  3. Link Subsequent Environments: Generate a "Link Code" from a secondary subaccount (for example Production) and use it within the primary development subaccount to establish a trusted connection between the environments.
  4. Promote to Production: Once linked, create a new Modeled Event Mesh for the production stage, connect the corresponding broker, and promote the validated application configurations to the live environment.

I hope this introduction to "Config Push" has provided you some valuable and useful insights. 

Feel free to comment any questions and remarks!

entry.title: Two SDI Errors That Will Ruin Your Friday: Cleanup Environment Prohibited and Persistent Data Store
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE  |  SAP SMART DATA INTEGRATION

Two SDI Errors That Will Ruin Your Friday (And How to Fix Both)

A field guide to debugging "Cleanup Environment Prohibited" and "Persistent Data Store Full" in SAP HANA Smart Data Integration — with the exact SQL that solves both.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Two of the most disruptive SDI errors in production aren't caused by bad code — they're caused by state that accumulates silently until it blocks everything. This post covers both: how to diagnose them, the exact SQL to run, and the manual cleanup steps that actually work.

The Setup

SAP Smart Data Integration (SDI) is one of the most powerful data movement capabilities in the HANA ecosystem. It's also one of the least forgiving when it comes to operational hygiene. Unlike a failed SQL query that you fix and re-run, SDI errors tend to be stateful — they reflect accumulated conditions in the replication engine that don't resolve themselves and don't always surface obvious diagnostic paths.

This article covers two errors that I've encountered in production SDI environments and that I see regularly in community questions without complete answers: the Cleanup Environment Prohibited error and the Persistent Data Store Full error. Both are solvable. Neither is well documented end-to-end. Here's exactly what to do.

ERROR 1 OF 2 

Cleanup Environment Prohibited

What You See

SDI Error: Cleanup of the replication environment is currently prohibited.

This error surfaces when you attempt to drop, reset, or clean up a remote subscription — or when a failed replication task leaves the SDI agent and HANA in an inconsistent state. The engine is telling you it cannot safely clean up because it believes there is active state it would be destroying.

The frustrating reality is that the 'active state' it's protecting is often already broken. Subscriptions that failed mid-replication, orphaned triggers on the source system, and remote subscription metadata that no longer corresponds to a real replication task can all cause this. HANA won't let you clean up until you prove the state is safe to remove — and doing that requires manual interrogation.

Step 1 — Understand What You're Looking At

Before running any cleanup statements, establish a clear picture of the current state. Start with your column counts to identify which tables have problems:

-- Check column counts to identify tables with anomalies

-- Tables with unexpectedly low column counts often indicate

-- incomplete replication or schema mismatch after a failed load

SELECT COUNT(column_name), table_name

FROM table_columns

WHERE schema_name LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

GROUP BY table_name

ORDER BY COUNT(column_name) ASC;

Tables appearing at the top of this result (lowest column counts) are your first area of investigation. A table that should have 80 columns showing 3 is a sign that the subscription was interrupted during initial load and the schema was only partially applied.

Next, check the current state of all remote subscriptions in the affected schema:

-- View all remote subscriptions — check STATUS and ERROR_MESSAGE columns

SELECT *

FROM M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

ORDER BY SUBSCRIPTION_NAME ASC;

Pay attention to the STATUS column. You are looking for subscriptions in ERROR, INITIALIZING, or SUSPENDED states — these are the ones blocking cleanup. A subscription in ACTIVE state with a healthy last_processed timestamp is not your problem. Everything else is a candidate for reset.

Cross-reference with record counts to confirm which tables are genuinely populated vs. which are empty shells from failed initializations:

-- Compare record counts — run in both source and target to identify gaps

SELECT table_name, record_count

FROM m_tables

WHERE schema_name LIKE '<your_schema_name>'

ORDER BY TABLE_NAME ASC;

Step 2 — Generate and Run the RESET Statements

Once you know which subscriptions need to be reset, generate the ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET statements. Running these manually for large schemas is error-prone — generate them programmatically:

-- Generate ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET for all affected subscriptions

-- Copy output and execute in sequence

SELECT

  'ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION '

  || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || SUBSCRIPTION_NAME

  || ' RESET;'

FROM M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<your_schema_name>';

Execute the generated statements. The format for each individual reset is:

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "<schema_name>"."<subscription_name>" RESET;

Important: RESET moves the subscription back to a state where HANA believes it is safe to clean up. It does not drop the subscription or delete data. Think of it as telling the engine: this subscription is no longer in active use, you can safely proceed with cleanup operations.

Step 3 — Clean Up the Source System

This is the step most documentation skips entirely. The Cleanup Environment Prohibited error often persists even after running RESET statements because orphaned objects remain on the source system. SDI creates triggers and shadow tables on the source to capture change data — if these aren't removed, the engine sees residual ownership and refuses full cleanup.

Run the following on the source system to identify what SDI left behind:

-- Find all objects owned by the SDI replication user

SELECT * FROM SYS.OWNERSHIP

WHERE OWNER_NAME LIKE '<sdi_user_name>%'

ORDER BY OWNER_NAME DESC;

-- Generate DROP statements for SDI-created triggers

SELECT 'DROP TRIGGER ' || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || TRIGGER_NAME || ';'

FROM TRIGGERS

WHERE OWNER_NAME LIKE '<sdi_user_name>%';

For the shadow tables (HADP% tables) that SDI creates in the source schema, generate DROP statements with CASCADE to handle any dependent objects:

-- Generate DROP statements for SDI shadow tables

-- CASCADE handles dependent views or synonyms

SELECT

  'CONNECT ' || SCHEMA_NAME || ' PASSWORD <pwd>;'

  || CHR(10) ||

  'DROP TABLE ' || SCHEMA_NAME || '.' || TABLE_NAME || ' CASCADE;'

FROM TABLES

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<source_schema>%'

AND TABLE_NAME LIKE 'HADP%';

Verify the shadow tables are actually present before running drops — confirm with a SELECT first:

-- Verify HADP shadow tables exist before dropping

SELECT * FROM TABLES

WHERE SCHEMA_NAME LIKE '<source_schema>%'

AND TABLE_NAME LIKE 'HADP%';

-- Also check for HADP-related triggers that may remain

SELECT * FROM TRIGGERS

WHERE TRIGGER_NAME LIKE '%HADP%'

AND TRIGGER_NAME LIKE '%LAST%'

ORDER BY SCHEMA_NAME DESC;

After completing source system cleanup, return to HANA and verify M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS shows no remaining subscriptions for the affected schema in ERROR or SUSPENDED state. If residual subscriptions remain, re-run the RESET generation query and execute again.

ERROR 2 OF 2 

Persistent Data Store Full

What You See

SDI Error: The persistent data store used by the Data Provisioning Agent is full.

This error is more operationally straightforward than the first, but it stops replication completely and tends to arrive without much warning. The persistent data store is a local storage area maintained by the SDI Data Provisioning Agent to buffer change data captured from source systems before it's applied to HANA. When this store fills up, the agent can no longer queue new changes and replication halts.

The common causes are a burst of change data volume that exceeds the normal buffer capacity, a replication lag that allowed the queue to grow unchecked, and — most commonly in my experience — subscriptions that stopped consuming from the data store while the source kept writing to it.

Step 1 — Diagnose Which Remote Source Is the Culprit

Before taking any action, identify exactly which remote source is consuming the most persistent data store space. This single query gives you the complete picture:

-- Check persistent data store usage by remote source

-- SIZE is in bytes — divide by 1073741824 for GB

SELECT

  REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME,

  SUM(SIZE) AS TOTAL_SIZE_BYTES,

  ROUND(SUM(SIZE) / 1073741824, 2) AS TOTAL_SIZE_GB

FROM REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS

GROUP BY REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME

ORDER BY TOTAL_SIZE_BYTES DESC;

The remote source at the top of this result is your primary offender. In most cases you will find one source consuming a disproportionate share — this is almost always a subscription that has stalled while the source continues generating change data.

Drill into the specific containers for that source to understand the composition:

-- Get container-level detail for the problematic remote source

SELECT *

FROM REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS

WHERE REMOTE_SOURCE_NAME = '<your_remote_source_name>'

ORDER BY SIZE DESC;

Cross-reference the container names against M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS to identify which subscriptions are associated with the largest containers and what their current replication status is.

Step 2 — Resolution Path

The resolution depends on what you found in Step 1. There are two scenarios:

Scenario A — The subscription is still valid and you want to keep it running. The data store is full because replication has lagged, not because the subscription is broken. In this case, the priority is to allow the agent to catch up by temporarily reducing the change data capture rate or pausing other subscriptions sharing the same agent, then monitoring the store size as the backlog clears.

Scenario B — The subscription is broken, stale, or no longer needed. This is the more common production scenario. Run the RESET and cleanup sequence from Error 1 for the affected subscriptions, which will release the data store containers associated with them. After the subscriptions are reset and dropped, the persistent data store space is reclaimed by the agent.

Monitoring recommendation: After resolving the immediate issue, set up a recurring alert on REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS size. A threshold alert at 70% capacity gives you enough runway to investigate before replication halts. The query from Step 1 is exactly what you need for a monitoring job — schedule it and route results to your operations team.

Quick Reference: Side-by-Side Comparison

 

Cleanup Environment Prohibited

Persistent Data Store Full

Root cause

Orphaned subscription state + source system residue

SDI agent buffer filled by lagging or stalled subscription

First query to run

M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS — check STATUS column

REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS — check SIZE by source

Primary fix

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION RESET + source cleanup

Reset stale subscriptions OR let active subscription catch up

Source system action needed?

Yes — drop triggers and HADP shadow tables

No — HANA-side resolution only in most cases

Recurrence prevention

Implement subscription state monitoring job

Alert at 70% persistent data store capacity

 

What Both Errors Have in Common

The pattern underlying both of these errors is the same: SDI maintains state that HANA itself cannot automatically reconcile. Unlike a failed query that leaves no trace, a failed replication task leaves orphaned objects, stalled containers, and subscription metadata that persists until you manually clear it. The engine's caution is appropriate — it won't clean up state it isn't certain is safe to remove. Your job as the architect is to prove that safety through the diagnostic sequence above.

The most important operational takeaway is that both errors are easier to prevent than to fix. A scheduled monitoring job that checks M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS for non-ACTIVE statuses and REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS for size thresholds will catch both conditions before they become production incidents. The queries in this article are exactly what you need for that monitoring layer.

If you're hitting either of these errors and the steps above aren't resolving them, the most useful thing to include when asking for help is the full output of M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS for your schema and the REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_DATA_CONTAINERS query result. Those two outputs contain almost everything needed to diagnose the specific variant you're dealing with.

entry.title: Re: Sneak Peek in to SAP Analytics Cloud release for Q1 2026
entry.content:
Hello @Orla_C ,

My question is about Story versioning. I have tried to use it, and it works well for one file. However, all versions disappears when copying the story. When such a story is "Save as" then versions are lost as well.

In our use case, developers copy a productive story in a separate folder, they change it there, and publish it back when the story is ready. In this scenario, versions disappear at the moment of copying or saving as.

If there is a way to use versions in this scenario, please let me know.

Br, Oleksandr

entry.title: Re: Send reminders to Onboardees to complete pending tasks
entry.content: Hi Akarsh, my Business scenario is to trigger email reminders to New Recruit from the Date Review New Hire is complete until they complete the Personal Data Collection, Frequency every day. I have used Filter Email Template. Content Builder - Onboarding Process Task Content Builder and Onboarding Process Task Role Based Recipient Builder. But its not working.

entry.title: Re: Is It Possible To Add Subtotal & total Button In Cust...
entry.content: Thanks Chirs, when we download it in excel can we extract the total subtotal data as it is in excel in Public cloud, could you please suggest your thoughts on this.Thanks

entry.title: Automatic Sales Order Creation From PO Data Which Available In PDF Format Over Email
entry.content:
Hello,

Customer send PO in PDF format over email on daily basis as per requirement. Now the requirement is to create automatic sales order by fetching the PO data.

Is this possible in SAP Public cloud? IF yes how can achieve this, please guide.

Regards,

Ashish

entry.title: EPMContextMember removing dimension from axis if user is not authorized
entry.content:
Hi all,

I am facing an issue with a BPC EPM 10.0 Input Form which I cannot figure out.

The Input Form includes among others a COUNTRY dimension managed by EPMContextMember and is accessed by users from several different countries that have been granted different roles to access their own Country only. It is saved in a BPC folder with one Country set by default, but when users that don't have the role for that particular Country open the Input Form the error "The following members of the page axis are not recognized as members and have been removed from the report: <ID of default Country in the report>" is shown before they can select their own Country or refresh.

This removes the Country dimension from the report entirely making it unusable since even if users select their Country they can't add the dimension back into the page axis.

Anyone has experienced a similar issue? Is there a way I can save the report without having to grant permission to all of the users for all the countries? Do I need to make one version for every single Country?

KR

I would appreciate any ideas or suggestions you may have.
 
Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Security breaches in FTP - SAP Communication
entry.content:
Hi @SheenaCarberry_10 -

 
When a security vulnerability or incident is identified on SAP BTP, SAP investigates, remediates, and deploys fixes for the platform and SAP‑managed services. Security vulnerabilities are communicated primarily via SAP Security Notes and HotNews, published on SAP Security Patch Day or out‑of‑cycle when required. Confirmed security incidents are governed and disclosed through the SAP Trust Center. If a security issue requires customer action—such as configuration changes or credential rotation—this is explicitly documented, and customers are responsible for implementation under the shared responsibility model.

The Shared Responsibility Model for SAP BTP is shown in SAP Help here: Shared Responsibility Model Between You and SAP | SAP Help Portal. SAP secures and patches the platform it operates, and customers/partners secure what they build, configure, and operate on top of it.

 
SAP does not publish a single whitepaper that maps the entire BTP security incident lifecycle end‑to‑end. Instead, the process is formally defined across SAP Trust Center incident management documentation, SAP Security Notes and Patch Day guidance, and the SAP BTP Shared Responsibility Model. Together, these documents define how SAP detects, remediates, and communicates security issues.
 
The first place I would start is setting up notifications specific to Security.

SAP notifications are configured individually per S-user. The S-User must be entitled to see the relevant customer landscape. This is based on the SAP user admin concept. There is not a general security distribution list customers and partners can subscribe to.

What I have seen other customers do is have at least two individual S-users where they save the same filter in order to be notified about Security HotNews. You want both the partner contacts and customer contacts set up these notifications to support redundancy. Below are the basic steps:

  1. Each person needs an S-user with the right authorizations. See About the User, Authorization and Administrator Concept - Search on the word "security" for the specific authorizations.
  2. Define a standard filter - like HotNews and components.
  3. Ask each S-User to subscribe using the same filter criteria set in Step 2.
  4. Ensure at least two S-Users are subscribed for continuity.
  5. Each relevant S-user needs to "turn on" SAP Hot News notifications. SAP Note 2530034 - How to manage e-mail, SMS, and/or SAP for Me notifications SAP for Me shows how.

A quick word of caution about creating filters - be careful not to "over-filter" on specific product components. Since SAP HotNews can sometimes share infrastructure components that may cause a missed notification. If you want specific product component notifications, consider setting up a separate filter for product specific news.

I hope this helps.

entry.title: Master Data Readiness: The Foundation for S/4HANA Transformation and AI Success
entry.content:
Why Data Readiness is No Longer Optional

In the rush to modernise ERP landscapes and adopt AI-driven insights, organisations often overlook a fundamental truth: the success of your S/4HANA transformation and AI initiatives is directly proportional to the quality of your master data. Poor data quality doesn't just slow down migration projects, it can derail AI use cases before they even begin and undermine the business value of your entire digital transformation investment.

As SAP Architects, we've seen the pattern repeatedly: organisations invest millions in S/4HANA implementations and AI platforms, only to discover that inconsistent customer records, duplicate vendor entries, and incomplete material master data render their advanced analytics and AI models ineffective. The promise of intelligent automation and predictive insights remains just that a promise when built on a foundation of fragmented, unreliable data.

  • 85% of AI projects fail to deliver promised value (Gartner)
  • $3.1 trillion annually lost due to poor data quality (IBM)
  • Clear link between data quality and AI failure
  • 40% of business initiatives fail due to data quality issues (MIT Sloan Management Review)

The stakes have never been higher. As organisations race to leverage AI for competitive advantage, master data readiness has shifted from a technical necessity to a strategic imperative.

The Master Data Challenge in S/4HANA Transformations

S/4HANA transformations expose master data issues that may have been hidden or tolerated in legacy ECC environments. The simplified data model, real-time processing capabilities, and integrated analytics of S/4HANA demand a level of data quality and consistency that many organisations simply don't have.

Common Master Data Issues We Encounter:

Data Quality Problems:

  • Inconsistent naming conventions across entities (customers, materials, vendors)
  • Incomplete or missing critical attributes
  • Duplicate records with no clear system of record
  • Inaccurate hierarchies and organisational structures
  • Outdated or obsolete data cluttering the system

Data Governance Gaps:

  • Unclear ownership and accountability for data domains
  • Lack of standardised processes for data creation and maintenance
  • No enforcement of data quality rules
  • Multiple systems maintaining conflicting versions of truth
  • Absence of data stewardship roles and responsibilities

Integration Complexity:

  • Master data scattered across SAP and non-SAP systems
  • No unified view of critical business entities
  • Manual reconciliation processes prone to errors
  • Point-to-point integrations creating data silos
  • Legacy systems unable to provide clean data feeds

These challenges don't disappear when you move to S/4HANA they multiply. And when you layer AI initiatives on top of poor-quality data, you're essentially teaching your algorithms to learn from and perpetuate your data problems.

Why Master Data Quality is the Foundation of AI Success

Here's the reality that many organisations discover too late: AI is only as intelligent as the data it learns from. Machine learning models trained on incomplete, inconsistent, or biased data will produce unreliable predictions. Natural language processing applied to unstructured data with poor quality metadata will fail to deliver meaningful insights.

The Cost of Poor Data in AI:

  • AI models that produce unreliable predictions, eroding business trust
  • Bias amplification when training data contains historical data quality issues
  • Inability to achieve expected ROI on AI investments
  • Manual intervention required to correct AI outputs, defeating automation goals
  • Delayed or abandoned AI initiatives due to data readiness gaps
  • Compliance and ethical risks from biased or incomplete training data

The harsh truth: you cannot AI-transform your way out of a data quality problem. Clean, governed, and unified master data is not a nice-to-have it's the prerequisite for AI success.

 

The Hidden Complexity: Unifying SAP and Non-SAP Data

One of the most underestimated challenges in achieving data readiness is the distributed nature of enterprise data. Critical master data doesn't live in SAP alone it's scattered across:

Common Non-SAP Master Data Sources:

CRM Systems, PLM Platforms, E-commerce Platforms, MDM Solutions, Procurement Systems and homegrown Applications to name a few.

 

The Integration Challenge

For S/4HANA transformations, this distributed landscape creates significant complexity:

  • Multiple versions of truth for the same business entity
  • No single system owns the golden record
  • Manual reconciliation required before migration
  • Risk of migrating conflicting or duplicate data
  • Difficulty tracing data lineage and transformation logic

For AI initiatives, the challenge is even more acute:

  • AI models require comprehensive data across all systems
  • Training data must represent the complete business context
  • Incomplete data leads to biased or unreliable models
  • Real-time AI use cases need synchronised data across systems
  • Data scientists spend most of their time on data preparation instead of model development
  •  

Building the Foundation: A Unified Approach to Master Data

The solution requires a unified master data strategy that brings together SAP and non-SAP data into a single, governed, and trusted source of truth. This foundation serves both your S/4HANA transformation and your AI journey.

Key Components of a Unified Master Data Strategy:

  1. 1. Centralised Master Data Management

Establish a central hub for managing critical business entities:

  • Single authoritative source for customers, products, suppliers, and financial master data
  • Unified data model that spans SAP and non-SAP systems
  • Golden record creation through intelligent matching and merging
  • Real-time synchronisation to downstream systems
  • API-first architecture for AI and analytics consumption
  1. Data Quality and Governance Framework

Implement systematic data quality management:

  • Automated data quality rules and validation checks
  • Duplicate detection and resolution workflows
  • Data standardisation and enrichment processes
  • Workflow-driven governance with clear ownership
  • Continuous monitoring and quality KPIs
  • Data stewardship programs with accountability

Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

Pitfall 1: Underestimating Data Cleansing Effort

The Problem: Organisations allocate less of project time to data cleansing when it realistically requires lots more.

Pitfall 2: Lack of Business Ownership

The Problem: Treating master data as an IT problem rather than a business accountability.

Pitfall 3: Ignoring Non-SAP Systems

The Problem: Focusing solely on SAP data while critical master data resides in other solutions.

Pitfall 4: "We'll Clean It Up After Go-Live"

The Problem: Deferring data quality issues to post-implementation, where they're exponentially harder and more expensive to address.

Pitfall 5: Treating Master Data and AI as Separate Initiatives

The Problem: Running master data improvement and AI development as independent projects with different teams and timelines.

Pitfall 6: Building Point Solutions Instead of Platforms

The Problem: Creating custom data quality tools and integration scripts that become technical debt and can't scale to AI demands.

 

The Business Case: Why Data Readiness Pays

While master data initiatives require investment, the ROI is compelling when you consider both S/4HANA and AI value realisation:

Quantifiable Benefits:

  • 30-40% reduction in S/4HANA migration timeline and risk
  • 50-70% improvement in AI model accuracy and reliability
  • 25-35% reduction in manual data reconciliation efforts
  • 60% faster time-to-deployment for new AI use cases
  • Elimination of duplicate payments and customer records
  • 20-30% reduction in data-related support incidents

Strategic Benefits:

  • Foundation for data-driven and AI-powered decision making
  • Enablement of real-time analytics and predictive insights
  • Improved customer experience through unified views and personalisation
  • Regulatory compliance and audit readiness for AI governance
  • Competitive advantage through faster, more reliable AI innovation
  • Reduced risk of AI bias and ethical issues from poor data

AI-Specific Value:

  • Data scientists spend less time on data preparation, more on model development
  • Higher model accuracy translates to better business outcomes
  • Faster experimentation and iteration on AI use cases
  • Reduced risk of AI project failure due to data quality issues
  • Ability to scale AI across the enterprise with confidence

The question isn't whether you can afford to invest in master data readiness it's whether you can afford not to, given the risks to your S/4HANA transformation and the potential lost value from underperforming AI initiatives.

 

Summary: The Path Forward

Master data readiness is not a one-time project it's a continuous discipline that requires the right strategy, platforms, processes, and people. As S/4HANA transformations and AI initiatives converge, the quality of your master data becomes the single most important factor in determining success or failure.

Key Takeaways:

  1. Data Quality is the AI Foundation - Poor master data quality will undermine AI initiatives regardless of how sophisticated your models are. Garbage in, garbage out applies more than ever.
  2. Unify SAP and Non-SAP Data - AI requires comprehensive data across your entire enterprise. Build a unified master data strategy that spans all systems, not just SAP.
  3. Start Early, Start Strategic - Data readiness must begin before S/4HANA migration and AI deployment, not after. Build the foundation correctly from day one.
  4. Governance is Non-Negotiable - Technology alone won't solve data quality problems. Establish clear ownership, stewardship, and accountability for data quality.
  5. Think Platform, Not Point Solutions - Invest in enterprise master data management platforms that can scale with your S/4HANA and AI ambitions. Solutions like SAP Business Data Cloud can help accelerate your AI journey by providing the unified, governed data foundation AI demands.
  6. Measure What Matters - Establish data quality KPIs and track AI model performance improvements attributable to better data. Make the business case visible and ongoing.
  7. Integration is Key - Build robust integration architecture that can handle real-time AI demands while maintaining data quality and governance across SAP and non-SAP systems.

 

Conclusion

The convergence of S/4HANA transformations and AI adoption creates both unprecedented opportunity and a critical dependency: the quality of your master data. Organisations that recognise this dependency and invest in comprehensive master data readiness will accelerate their transformations, de-risk their migrations, and unlock the full potential of AI-driven insights and automation.

Those that continue to treat master data as an afterthought will find themselves trapped in a cycle of rework, delayed value realisation, and unreliable AI outputs regardless of how much they invest in cutting-edge technology.

The foundation of digital transformation and AI success isn't S/4HANA or machine learning platforms it's the quality, consistency, and accessibility of your master data across your entire enterprise landscape. Whether you leverage SAP Business Data Cloud, another master data management solution, or build your own platform, the imperative remains the same: unified, governed, AI-ready master data is non-negotiable.

For AI initiatives specifically, the data readiness challenge is even more acute. AI models are only as good as the data they learn from. Clean, comprehensive, and well-governed master data across SAP and non-SAP systems isn't just nice to have it's the difference between AI that transforms your business and AI that disappoints.

The question for SAP Architects is simple: are you ready to make master data readiness the strategic priority it needs to be for both your S/4HANA transformation and your AI journey?

The organisations that answer "yes" and act decisively will be the ones leading their industries with AI-powered innovation built on a foundation of trusted data. The others will be left wondering why their expensive AI investments failed to deliver and the answer will be staring them in the face: they skipped the foundation.

 

The window of competitive advantage is closing. Organisations that built their data foundation correctly are already deploying AI at scale improving customer experiences, optimising operations, and making better decisions faster. Those still struggling with basic data quality aren't just behind on AI adoption they're watching the gap widen every quarter.

AI doesn't wait for you to fix your data. Your competitors who prioritised data quality are already leveraging AI to eat your market share.

The choice is stark: invest in master data readiness now and unlock AI's potential or continue treating data quality as "someone else's problem" and watch your AI initiatives fail one by one.

SAP is Here to Help

Recognising that master data readiness is a critical challenge for organisations embarking on S/4HANA and AI journeys, SAP offers dedicated services designed to guide you through this transformation:

SAP Data Readiness Discovery Workshop

SAP Business Data Cloud Discovery Workshop

These workshops provide the expertise, frameworks, and actionable insights to accelerate your master data journey. SAP's experienced Architects work alongside your teams to assess your unique challenges, design practical solutions, and establish the data foundation that both S/4HANA and AI success demand.

You don't have to navigate the complexity of master data readiness alone. SAP is here to help and advice customers on building the data foundation that unlocks the full potential of digital transformation and AI innovation.

Ready to begin your master data readiness journey? Reach out to your SAP account team to learn more about these discovery workshops and how SAP can support your organisation's path to data excellence.

entry.title: Advanced Production Integration | Basic or Advanced EWM license?
entry.content:
Dear community,

Within SAP EWM, there are two approaches to production integration:

  1. Delivery-Based Production Integration

  2. Advanced Production Integration (based on Production Material Requests – PMRs)

We would like to clarify the licensing requirements for these two options.

Do both approaches fall under the basic EWM license, or is the advanced EWM license required in order to use Advanced Production Integration (option 2)?

The SAP documentation appears somewhat unclear on this point, as it generally refers to the broader term “production integration” as being included in the basic license, without explicitly distinguishing between the two approaches.

I would really appreciate some clarification on this matter.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Interview Scheduling for Experts
entry.content: Do you mean giving the Interviewers the option to select their slots availability, before the candidate is invited ? I think the intention of Interview Scheduling is that the interviewer would either have made slots available in their Interview scheduling "My Calendar" - if you are not using Outlook integration. Or that an offline conversation would have happened with the interviewer to agree which times from his diary (if you are using outlok integration) that they want to have the interviews in. Then the recruiter / organiser proffers those agreed "Slots" to the candidates.

entry.title: KI-Kompetenzentwicklung und Weiterbildung - Praxiserfahrungen und bewährte Ansätze
entry.content:
In der SAP Training und Change Management Community Session ging es im Februar um KI Kompetenzentwicklung. Diesesmal habe ich nicht moderiert, sondern selbst einen Impuls gegeben. 

Als Intro habe ich Befragungsergebnisse vorgestellt. Eine Studie von Stifterverband und McKinsey mit rund 1000 Firmen zeigt, dass bei fast 80 Prozent befragter deutscher Firmen grundlegende KI-Kenntnisse fehlen.

Danach stellte ich unser SAP AI Skill-Self Assessment vor. Die Auswertung zeigt: Motivation ist am höchsten, dann kommt Awareness. Beim Wissen zeigen sich Lücken, und die Implementierungskompetenz fällt am geringsten aus. Die hohe Motivation bildet eine gute Basis für die Weiterentwicklung von Awareness, Wissen und vor allem der praktischen Anwendungskompetenz. Details zur Auswertung findet Ihr hier. 

Meine sieben Tipps zur KI-Kompetenzentwicklung

Tipp 1: KI-Grundlagen für alle

Ich sehe grundlegende KI-Kenntnisse als essenziell für alle – vom Schüler bis zum SAP-Entwickler. In der SAP Community haben wir mit Kollegen eine kuratierte Liste von SAP AI Trainings erstellt, die in etwa einem Tag absolvierbar ist. Die Grundlagenkurse decken Joule Agents, Business AI allgemein und Responsible AI Themen wie Ethik ab. 

Formelles Lernen durch E-Learning macht für Grundlagen absolut Sinn. Neben SAP-Angeboten empfehle ich Microsoft Akademien, DeepLearning.AI, Open HPI mit deutschsprachigen Onlinekursen oder den KI Campus. Viele Angebote sind kostenfrei. Bereits bei den Grundlagen ist der Austausch in Communities wichtig sowie das Ausprobieren.

Tipp 2: Selbsteinschätzung durchführen - Wo stehe ich?

Ich rate immer zu einer Standortbestimmung für sich selbst und das Team. Ein Self-Assessment für sich oder sein Team bietet eine einfache Selbsteinschätzung. Dahinter können verschiedene Kompetenzmodelle stehen.

Das Modell von Stifterverband und McKinsey unterscheidet zwischen Grundwissen, praktischer generativer KI-Kompetenz wie Prompting, Inhalteerstellung und Prozessautomatisierung sowie analytischen Kompetenzen. Die Modell- und Toolauswahl bleibt wichtig – es braucht ein Gefühl dafür, welches Tool für welche Herausforderung passt, ob Gemini, Claude oder SAP-Lösungen. Datengetriebenes Entscheiden und datenbasiertes Handeln gehören ebenso dazu wie die kritische ethische Einordnung von KI.

Das AI Competency Framework von Next Education von Professor Ehlers ist allgemeiner aufgestellt mit Future Skills für eine durch KI geprägte Lebenswelt. Es umfasst das Arbeiten und Gestalten mit und für KI als Umsetzungskompetenz, persönliche Fähigkeiten wie kreative Problemlösung sowie die Gestaltung des sozialen Umfelds mit ethischer und Kooperationskompetenz.

Mein pragmatisches Modell gliedert sich in technische, analytische, soziale, ethische und Veränderungskompetenz. Es folgt dem Schema Skills, Knowledge und Attitudes – man muss etwas wissen, etwas können, und bei manchen Themen wie Ethik geht es auch um Einstellungen und Haltung.

In der Session führten wir eine Live-Selbsteinschätzung per Mentimeter durch. Die Teilnehmenden ordneten sich bei verschiedenen SAP-KI-Themen zwischen Awareness und Practice ein und wir sahen, dass sich die meisten im Bereich Awareness verorten.

Tipp 3: Rollenbasierte Lernpläne für KI

Nach der Selbsteinschätzung sollte man sich die Frage stellen: Wo will ich hin? Ein Lernplan sollte nach Rolle und Qualifikation ausgerichtet sein. Die Anforderungen für Entwickler beispielsweise unterscheiden sich von denen für Beraterinnen.

In der SAP Community habe ich einen Blog mit einer Vorauswahl und Empfehlung für KI Lerninhalte erstellt. Es gibt hier rollenbasierte Angebote für Business User, Admins, Berater und technische KI-Lernangebote. Ein neuer Trainer-geführter Kurs "Applying Business AI Solutions in Finance" steht als Virtual Training Lab oder im Klassenzimmer zur Verfügung.

Auch Live-Sessions werden regelmäßig aktualisiert, derzeit gibt es 186 Sessions rund um KI-Themen. Übungssysteme zum Ausprobieren, Zertifizierungen wie für den GenAI Developer oder Positioning SAP Business AI für Presales und Beratung ergänzen das Angebot.

Tipp 4: Lernziele setzen und Fortschritt dokumentieren

Verbunden mit dem Punkt davor ist es hilfreich sich konkrete Lernziele zu setzen. Nicht nur "ich will KI lernen", sondern beispielsweise "bis Ende März die drei Joule Grundkurse absolvieren, Projekte umsetzen und Blogbeiträge lesen".

Das Reflektieren und Teilen der Lernerfahrung unterstützt das Behalten und die Verankerung. Dies kann über Blogs, unsere LinkedIn Gruppe, die SAP Community Gruppe hier, Lerntagebücher oder Gespräche mit Kollegen erfolgen. Manche nutzen feste Termine, andere arbeiten selbstgesteuert.

Im Chat sammelten wir kurz Ergebnisse zu den letzten Lernerfahrungen. Die Teilnehmenden teilten: Nutzung von Microsoft Copilot, Synthesia für Videocontent und Trainingsinhalten, KI als Sparringpartner bei der Konzepterstellung, AI Agents, AI Content Production, Kulturautobiografie via AI Video und Podcasts.

Tipp 5: Mit und von anderen lernen

Ich bin seit Jahren Fan von Peer Learning über Formate wie Barcamps, Workshops, Lerngruppen, Promptatons, Communities und Live-Sessions. Der Austausch mit anderen unterstützt die Kompetenzentwicklung durch Reflexion und unterschiedliche Perspektiven.

Promptatons als Lightweight-Hackathon

Promptatons sind ein niedrigschwelliges Format, eine Art Lightweight-Hackathon von zwei Stunden bis zu einem Tag, hybrid, virtuell oder vor Ort. Bei SAP finden Beispielsweise Sessions mit über 100 Personen virtuell statt.

Das Format besteht aus minimalem Input zu gutem Prompting und Tools die man nutzen kann, dann folgen Challenges aus der Lebensrealität der Teilnehmenden. Mindestens eine Stunde, besser eineinhalb Stunden arbeiten Kleingruppen von drei bis vier Personen hands-on, unterstützt durch Coaches. Am Ende erfolgt ein Report-back zu Lessons Learned und Lösungswegen.

Die Challenges sollten aus der Praxis stammen und in Schwierigkeitsstufen verfügbar sein. Level 1 wäre beispielsweise das Designen von Personas, Zusammenfassen von Videos oder Schreiben von Blogs. Schwierigere Herausforderungen wären  Agenten oder GPTs bauen, etwa einen Lernbegleiter. Stretch-Goals fordern zusätzlich heraus.

Hier ein Beispiel: Innovative Idea generation (Level 2)

Create a prompt to brainstorm innovative ideas for a new product or service. Simulate a brainstorming session that generate ideas based on customer needs, industry trends etc. The final output should include a list of ideas with their potential impact.
Stretch Goal: Rank these ideas based on feasibility, viability, and desirability.
Tools to use: xy tool accessible for the group (or bring your own AI Tool). 
Wichtig ist ein digitales Whiteboard oder Brown Paper vor Ort, Selbstorganisation der Gruppen und Coaches mit Tool- und Prompting-Expertise. Die Reflexion des Lösungswegs sind hilfreich. Viele wünschen sich längeren Zugriff auf die erarbeiteten Prompts. 
Firmen wie Continental, Telekom, DM setzen Promptatons ein, um konkrete Anwendungsfälle aus zu probieren.

Communities und Lerngruppen

In der SAP Community gibt es die u.a. die AIDeveloper Community Gruppe, die AI Learning Group oder eben die Gruppe hier zu Lernen, Change & Transformation. Dort finden sich immer Blogs und Diskussionen im Forum. Man kann dabei durch eigene Fragen und durch das Beantworten von Fragen anderer lernen. Zusätzlich gibt es unterschiedliche Events zum Austausch.

Die neue AI Learning Group teilt vor allem neue Kurse, kuratiert von einem SAP Experten. Marc Green, ein erfahrener SAP-Mastertrainer, moderiert ebenfalls eine Gruppe und erklärt Themen in kurzen Videos mit viel Erfahrungswissen.

Lernleitfäden für kleine Lerngruppen sind eine weitere Möglichkeit. Die Learning Circle Experience zu Change Management funktionierte in Kleingruppen mit einem Leitfaden voller Impulse und Übungen. Der LernOS Lernleitfaden für generative KI kann von Teams oder Lerngruppen genutzt werden. Die Erfahrungen zeigen, dass manche auch die Termine komprimieren. So kann man sich statt drei Monate wöchentlich beispielsweise drei bis vier mal komprimiert treffen. Die Leitfäden verbinden das Lernen von und miteinander, sind relativ kostenneutral und haben das Element der sozialen Unterstützung und des Austausches. 

Tipp 6: Durch eigene Erfahrung und Handeln lernen

KI versteht man nur, indem man es nutzt. Bei einer breiten und mächtigen Technologie muss jeder kontextualisieren und prüfen, wo KI bei den eigenen Aufgaben hilft, wo man kreativer oder effektiver wird. Experimentierräume schaffen und erkunden ist wichtig. So kann man sich Lernprojekten vornehmen, um ein konkretes Problem mit KI zu lösen.

SAP AppHaus Methoden

Die SAP AppHaus Methoden bieten verschiedene Tools rund um KI. Der AI Exploration Workshop prüft Use Cases nach dem Dreiklang aus Design Thinking: Feasibility, Viability und Desirability. Es gibt einen Leitfaden für einen Tag oder eine Mini-Version für zwei Stunden. Weitere Vorlagen zur Agent Discovery und Agent Design führen einen Schritt weiter.

Diese Workshops können strategisch top-down umgesetzt werden. Ich empfehle zudem, dass jedes Team einen Business AI Exploration Workshop durchführt und gemeinsam AI Anwendungsfälle exploriert. 

Anbei die Chat-Ergebnisse zu Lieblings-Use-Cases der Teilnehmenden: ChatGPT für Programmierung, Zusammenfassung von To-Dos aus Meetings, Workshopvorbereitung, Konzepte, kritisches Feedback zu Konzepten, Copilot für Einsatzpläne von Azubis, Agenten für Onboarding-Fragen, Perplexity für Recherche, Textarbeit wie Vorbereitung, Erstellung, Strukturierung und Personalisierung, Daily Digest zur Strukturierung von Outlook. Ein Beispiel aus meiner Praxis: Bei Podcasts unterstützt KI bei Skriptvorbereitung, Recherche, Audio-to-Text-Transkription, Zusammenfassung als Newsletter, Covergestaltung und Bildbearbeitung.

Phasen beim AI Rollout war darauf eine Frage im Chat. Ich empfehle, Grundlagen und Anwendung parallel zu verbinden. Einfache Use Cases wie E-Mail schreiben, Meetings zusammenfassen oder Recherche kann man früh integrieren, statt eine große Schulungswelle vorweg zu machen ohne konkrete Anwendung. Bei KI Grundlagenschulungen ohne jegliche Anwendung wird sonst nur das Meiste vergessen. 

Tipp 7: Regelmäßig KI Kenntnisse updaten

Lernroutinen des regelmässigen Updaten sind notwendig, da KI sich schnell entwickelt. Der SAP AI-Newsletter, unsere Podcasts auf Apple, Spotify liefern solche regelmäßigen Updates. Veranstaltungen von SAP und Partnern, die TechEd für Entwickler und technisch Interessierte mit kostenfreien Inhalten sind weitere Quellen.

Ein Metaansatz ist die Erstellung von AI News Agenten, etwa mit ChatGPT oder Perplexity, die zu speziellen Themen recherchieren und täglich oder wöchentlich ein Digest mit Links und kritischer Einordnung liefern.

Mein Tipp: KI immer einen Platz am Tisch geben. Bei jedem neuen Thema sollte man überlegen, wie KI helfen kann, auch multimodal oder im Dialog.

Chat-Ergebnisse zu Update-Quellen waren: The Neuron Newsletter, One Useful Thing | Ethan Mollick, heise KI Update, Doppelgänger TechTalk, SAP Learning Newsletter, KI-Podcast der ARD, Carrie Speaker auf TikTok, LinkedIn mit Experten wie Dennis Hassabis von DeepMind, Snip KI Newsletter, Corporate Learning Camp, Mastodon, YouTube-Kanäle wie Matt Wolf.

Ergänzenden SAP-Angebote und Formate

SAP bietet Kunden Innovation Days für Business AI, Inspiration Sessions als zweitägige Workshops, Maturity Assessments durch das Advisory Team (früher Business Transformation Berater)  zur Standortbestimmung auf der KI-Reise (Kontakt gerne über mich). 

AI Exploration Workshops vom SAP Apphaus und die Tool-Aktivierungsmission für SuccessFactors oder Online-Formate mit Breakout-Sessions sind verfügbar.

Die Agent Days in Berlin die z.B. Ende Februar laufen umfassen zwei Tage mit Inspiration und Breakout-Sessions nach Industrie. Das Thema Agents bietet hohes Potenzial für Automatisierung, gerade mit Joule Studio im SAP-Kontext.

Hackathons in Berlin, Zürich und München dauern eine Woche, erfordern Bewerbung mit Use Cases und bieten enge Betreuung. Dieses hands-on Format ist aufwendig, aber durch die Konkretheit ein sehr starkes Lernformat.

Resüme, Diskussion und weitere Hinweise

Ein Teilnehmer merkte an, dass Context Engineering zwischen Content und Prompting als Kompetenz wichtig ist. Das ist wohl wahr: die Bedeutung von Kontext bei KI, z.B. mit Nutzung von RAG und eigener firmenrelevantem Daten ist absolut wichtig. Kompetenzmodelle sind vereinfachte Abbildungen der Realität und sollten flexibel angepasst werden.

Zusammenfassend ist für KI Kompetenz folgendes wichigt: Grundlagen verstehen, Selbsteinschätzung vornehmen, rollenbasierte Lernpläne erstellen, Ziele setzen und dokumentieren, Peer Learning nutzen, durch Handeln lernen und regelmäßig updaten.

Wir werden diese Themen weiter in der Community beleuchten. Weitere Kaffeeecken zu den Themen S/4 Transformationen, Change Readiness und Business Transformation Formaten sind geplant - ihr findet die Anmeldung wie immer auf der Webseite zur SAP Change & Training Kaffee-Ecke.

entry.title: Given a Customer Account set up as a ship-to partner function of another account find that sold-to
entry.content:
In SAP HANA I have a customer account, call it 1001234 - it represents a customer, with many ship-to locations. So it is my sold-to account number.

Also I have an account, call it 5009876 and it represents one specific ship-to location/address.

In 1001234, under "Sales and Distribution" in the Partner Functions table, I assign 5009876 as a Ship-To party of 1001234.

I do not maintain anything in the new "Relationships" section of 1001234

Is it possible, by looking at the screens for account 5009876 to know that it was assigned as a ship to partner function of 1001234?

This was possible in ECC using the "Customer Partner Functions" choice in the "Extras" menu when viewing 5009876.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Sales Order Creation From PO Data Which Ava...
entry.content:
Dear @Ashish13,

Option 1. Yes you can create Sales documents from PO PDFs

SAP provides standard apps to upload a customer PO PDF/image, extract fields, create a Sales Order Request and then convert it to a Sales Order:

  • Create Sales Orders Automatic Extraction (F4920), scope item 4X9 must be activated for the extraction scenario to be available in the system

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F4920')/S35

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/2dcf49a616b842b096e0a3cad4dac458.html

  • Create Sales Orders AI-Assisted Extraction (F8947_TRAN) for this app in addition to the required IAM app assignment/authorizations, availability may depend on the relevant AI/Generative AI entitlement being enabled for the tenant

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/10c773a027dd4293a78972d627990d83.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/565f8b8bd23944f1b04836a1b424c647.html

IMPORTANT: These apps are primarily file-upload driven not read emails automatically. Also the extraction scenario must be active SAP calls out issues when the intelligent scenario/model isn’t activated for example SO_AUTO_EXTRACTION

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3591344

Option 2. Fully automatic from inbox is not pure standard

To fetch email attachments and trigger creation automatically, you typically use BTP automation/integration for example:

Option A: SAP Build Process Automation (desktop bot / task automation)

SAP provides an automation concept specifically for Automatic Creation of Sales Order from Unstructured Data

More details below:

https://www.sap.com/assetdetail/2024/01/0217a7c3-a77e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUILD_PROCESS_AUTOMATION_DESKTOP_AGENT/eae9c11a17a14cfc93e08b22e8574305/145b77281a084a64ae03685a087ca84b.html

Option B: SAP Integration Suite

  • Use Cloud Integration Mail adapter to read mailbox + download attachments
  • Call Document Information Extraction for structured output
  • Create the sales order via released API_SALES_ORDER_SRV

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/mail-adapter

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/configure-mail-sender-adapter

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/document-information-extraction-process-automation

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/00d244581efca007e10000000a441470.html

Summary:

  • If you can accept a review/validation step start with F4920 / F8947_TRAN
  • If it must be touchless from email go with Build Process Automation (quickest) or Integration Suite and Sales Order API most robust/clean-core

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Route Map – No possibility to select “specific user”...
entry.content:
I have a somewhat similar scenario where my customer wants an iterative step between the Comp Admin and the Comp Planner because they want the Comp Admin to be the "exit user" and not allow the planner to push to next step.

In my case, we solved this by using "Initiator" and proxying in as the Comp Admin to launch worksheets (which we would do anyway even without the issue since we want worksheets to come from the CompAdmin user). This allowed us to have the Comp Admin be the "Initiator" for the iterative step.

If your scenario is not a situation where the "specific user" is someone who'd have access to launch worksheets, this solution doesn't work but I thought I'd mention it just in case. This doesn't work if the "specific" user is different for different worksheets.

entry.title: Re: Unable to edit plant address in DEV Customizing
entry.content:
Hi!

Access to Configuration Activities (SSCUI’s) within the SAP Central Business Configuration workspace is strictly dependent on the lifecycle phase of the workspace.

The SSCUI’s become available only when the workspace has progressed to the Product-Specific Configuration phase. If the workspace is still in the Scope and Organizational Structure phase, access to the configuration activities under Configure Your Business Processes is technically restricted by design.

To proceed, it is mandatory to execute the Deploy Scope and Organizational Structure activity within the workspace. This step triggers the deployment of the defined scope and organizational structure to the assigned target system.

Once the deployment process is successfully completed, the workspace automatically transitions to the Product-Specific Configuration phase. At that point, all relevant SSCUI’s under Configure Your Business Processes become accessible and available for maintenance and access.

Kindly check KBA 3658821 - Configuration activities are unavailable while the Organizational Structure is in progress in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me.

For additional information, please refer to:

  • Configure Your Business Processes | SAP Help Portal

Best Regards!

entry.title: Joule for S/4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Hello,

We are planning to integrate Joule with S/4HANA Private cloud.  Please clarify below questions:

Does customer have both options as below? or only option B?

A. Integrate Joule with Work Zone Site 

B. Integration Joule with S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad

 

SAP documentation ( https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/integrating-joule-with-sap/configure-destinations ) asks to create below destinations:

  1. Design time destination
  2. Runtime destination - Documentation says Authentication method= No Authentication. Is this wrong as we should have Principal Propagation for this destination ?
  3. Separate destination for retrieving SAP Card nominations - This destination has fixed nameIdFormat as tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress
    Does this mean we have to use only email address as attribute for principal propagation?
  4. Single Sign On destination (S4HANA_PCE_SSO): What is use of this destination? Why cant we leverage Runtime destination ?
  5. RFC destination for Identity Provisioning -
    Do we need to assign any roles to users in BTP? or only replication is needed?
    If customer has different Identity Governance solution to provision users in IAS/BTP, do we need this step?

Then documentation talks about BTP Work Zone setup only up to step of exposing the content. 

Is this required only for Joule to use navigational service? Does customer need to pay for users in Work Zone?

 

Regards,

VP

entry.title: Status of the study program with reassignment in LMS
entry.content:
Good afternoon, team.

I am writing to share the following issue I'm experiencing with the LMS module.

We are currently in the process of reassigning courses or articles for all company employees. It's important to note that this reassignment should occur every three years after completion.

However, when searching for a user and validating their assigned study programs, the program is registered as completed even though the three-year reassignment period has not yet ended. I've attached an image as evidence of this.

Good afternoon, team,

I am writing to share the following issue I'm experiencing with the LMS module.

We are currently in the process of reassigning courses or articles for all company employees. It's important to note that this reassignment should occur every three years after completion.

However, when searching for a user and validating their assigned study programs, the program is registered as completed even though the three-year reassignment period has not yet ended. I have attached an image as evidence.

The purpose of this inquiry is to determine if this is a normal behavior of the tool or if there is a way for the study program status to register as incomplete even after the three-year reassignment period has expired.

I look forward to your response.

Sincerely,

entry.title: Re: Customs VAT Supplier Invoice
entry.content:
Hello @sanne_larsen ,

Process

1. Create PO for foreign vendor
2. Post Goods Receipt
3. Receive customs invoice:
DUTY → planned/unplanned delivery cost (capitalized)
VAT → input VAT tax code (recoverable)
4. System posts:
Inventory increased by DUTY
VAT posted to input VAT G/L
5. VAT reported in Belgian VAT return

 

Situation / QuestionStandard Process in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition + SAP References
Goods imported from outside EU into BelgiumStandard process: 1️⃣ Post Goods Receipt against PO (inventory value based on supplier invoice). 2️⃣ Post customs duty as additional cost (planned or unplanned delivery cost) so it capitalizes inventory.
Customs invoice contains DUTY + VATThese must be treated separately: • DUTY → increases inventory value (capitalizable cost). • Import VAT → recoverable input VAT (tax receivable), not inventory cost.
How to post DUTYUse supplier invoice (customs broker) referencing PO. Post duty as planned delivery cost (condition type) or unplanned delivery cost. System posts: Dr Inventory Cr Vendor
How to post Import VATImport VAT is posted via tax code for input VAT (Belgium import VAT code). VAT is calculated on customs base value but is recoverable tax, not cost. System posts: Dr Input VAT (Tax G/L) Cr Vendor
Concern: Gross and VAT amounts are equalThis is normal for customs VAT invoices because VAT base = customs value. Even if VAT equals invoice amount, it is still tax-only posting. The system handles this via tax code calculation logic.
Is VAT added to inventory?❌ No. Import VAT is recoverable input VAT and must not increase inventory. Only DUTY capitalizes inventory.
Standard Posting LogicPO → GR → Customs Invoice (MIRO) with: • Delivery cost line (DUTY) • Tax code for Import VAT System automatically splits inventory and tax correctly.
If customs VAT invoice is separate documentPost FI supplier invoice with appropriate input VAT tax code (no PO reference required if VAT-only). The tax code ensures correct VAT reporting in Belgian VAT return.
Impact on VAT ReturnImport VAT appears as deductible input VAT in VAT report (Belgian VAT grid 59 depending on setup).
Avoid “creative accounting”Do not manually post VAT to expense or inventory. Always use tax code-based posting.
SAP Help DocumentationSAP Help Portal → Procurement → Invoice Processing → Delivery Costs SAP Help → Finance → Tax Management → Input Tax Posting SAP Help → Belgium Localization – VAT Reporting
Relevant SAP Notes (Check Release Applicability)📌 SAP Note 2222774 – Import VAT Handling in MM-IV 📌 SAP Note 2468156 – Delivery Costs Capitalization in Material Ledger 📌 SAP Note 2533962 – Tax Code Configuration for Input VAT in S/4HANA 📌 SAP Note 3090106 – VAT Reporting in S/4HANA Cloud

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh
